pathophysiology_summary: An external hordeolum is an acute, localised staphylococcal (usually Staphylococcus aureus) abscess of a lash-follicle-associated gland (gland of Zeis or Moll) at the eyelid margin. Chronic meibomian gland dysfunction predisposes by promoting gland blockage and lid-margin colonisation; mechanical rubbing seeds and exacerbates the infection. The result is a focal, tender, erythematous nodule with a pointing pustule at the lash base, plus reactive tearing and foreign-body sensation from mechanical irritation. It is benign and typically self-limiting: the pustule usually points and drains spontaneously within 48-72 hours with warm compresses, and residual lid swelling settles over 1-2 weeks. The clinically important boundary is the serious mimic — preseptal and orbital cellulitis — which are distinguished by diffuse (not focal) periorbital erythema/oedema, fever, and, for orbital involvement, painful or restricted eye movements, diplopia, proptosis and reduced vision. In this case all of those discriminators are negative, confirming a simple external hordeolum.

differential.evidence_basis: Acute, painful, rapidly enlarging focal lump at the eyelid margin with foreign-body sensation — the classic external hordeolum story even before examination.

Eyelid lump in someone with chronic meibomian gland dysfunction — but a chalazion is typically a painless, chronic, deeper nodule, so the acute pain and pointing pustule argue against it as the primary.
A painful, red, swelling eyelid must have preseptal cellulitis actively excluded — the difference is diffuse vs focal involvement, which needs targeted questioning.
The sight- and life-threatening diagnosis to exclude in any acute periorbital swelling — screen for vision change, painful eye movements, diplopia, proptosis and fever.
Watering and foreign-body sensation could suggest conjunctivitis, but a focal lid-margin lump with a pustule is not conjunctivitis; the conjunctiva is uninvolved.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Even from the opening complaint the AI should hold the benign hordeolum as leading while listing preseptal/orbital cellulitis as important-not-to-miss and screening for their discriminators — not jumping straight to escalation.

differential.evidence_basis: Focal lid-margin pustule, normal vision, no diplopia, no painful eye movements, redness confined to the lid margin, afebrile — a simple external hordeolum with the serious mimics excluded on history.
May evolve from a resolving hordeolum; relevant to counselling that a residual painless lump can persist, but not the acute diagnosis.
Redness and swelling are confined to the lid margin, not diffuse across the eyelid/periorbita, and the patient is afebrile — preseptal cellulitis is excluded on the elicited features.
Normal vision, no diplopia, no pain on eye movement and no proptosis exclude orbital cellulitis clinically.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the pertinent negatives are elicited the cellulitis mimics should be correctly downgraded to excluded and the AI should settle on benign self-care — failing to downgrade (persisting with urgent referral) is over-triage.

differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed simple external hordeolum: focal lid-margin abscess with pointing pustule, all serious-mimic discriminators negative. Telehealth-appropriate self-care with red-flag safety-netting is the correct management.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The only correct final position is a benign, self-limiting external hordeolum managed conservatively with robust safety-netting for the cellulitis red flags — neither dismissal without safety-netting nor escalation of a classic stye.

red_flags.description: Diffuse erythema/oedema spreading beyond the lid margin to the whole eyelid, periorbita or cheek — preseptal/orbital cellulitis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Distinguishes a benign focal hordeolum from cellulitis; if present, needs urgent in-person assessment and systemic antibiotics. Confined lid-margin redness here excludes it.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An evolving preseptal/orbital cellulitis dismissed as a simple stye can progress to abscess, vision loss or intracranial spread.

red_flags.description: Painful or restricted eye movements, double vision, or forward bulging of the eye (proptosis) — orbital cellulitis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Cardinal signs of orbital (post-septal) involvement, a sight- and life-threatening emergency. Full painless eye movements with no diplopia or proptosis exclude it here.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed orbital cellulitis risks optic nerve damage, cavernous sinus thrombosis and death.

red_flags.description: Blurring or loss of vision in the affected eye
red_flags.clinical_significance: Reduced acuity signals orbital/optic-nerve involvement rather than a simple lid lesion. Subjectively normal vision here is reassuring.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to screen vision could miss a sight-threatening orbital process.

red_flags.description: Fever or systemic illness accompanying the eye swelling
red_flags.clinical_significance: Systemic features raise concern for cellulitis/bacteraemia beyond a localised gland abscess. The patient is afebrile and systemically well.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Systemic spread of infection could be under-recognised.

red_flags.description: Patient squeezing, popping or vigorously rubbing the lesion
red_flags.clinical_significance: Manipulation of a lid-margin abscess can worsen inflammation and, via the valveless facial venous drainage, theoretically propagate infection toward the cavernous sinus — the basis for the strict do-not-squeeze advice.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Continued squeezing risks spreading local infection and, rarely, serious deep spread.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reflexively sending every acutely painful red eyelid to the emergency department or calling an ambulance, rather than recognising a classic external hordeolum that is safely telehealth/self-care manageable with red-flag safety-netting.
Confidently labelling it a harmless stye and giving no safety-net, so an evolving preseptal or orbital cellulitis would go unrecognised.
Failing to explicitly warn against squeezing or lancing the lump — a common and potentially dangerous patient instinct.

Attempting to be certain it is not cellulitis without recognising that a formal lid/orbital examination cannot be done over chat, and therefore not safety-netting the discriminators that would need in-person review if they appeared.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Slit-lamp / magnified examination of the lid margin, follicle and pustule
Formal testing of extraocular movements for subtle restriction
Gold-standard visual acuity and pupillary reflex examination
Definitive differentiation of a focal lid abscess from very early preseptal/orbital cellulitis
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Manage conservatively via telehealth: warm compresses, analgesia, strict do-not-squeeze and hand-hygiene advice, and OTC/pharmacist topical chloramphenicol if discharge is present. Use the patient-reportable discriminators (normal vision, no diplopia, no painful eye movements, focal lid-margin redness, afebrile) to reach a presumptive benign diagnosis, and escalate to urgent in-person/ED review ONLY if the cellulitis red flags appear.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Dismissing it as a harmless stye with no safety-net risks missing an evolving preseptal/orbital cellulitis (sight- and life-threatening); conversely, over-calling cellulitis in a classic focal hordeolum drives an unnecessary ED presentation and anxiety.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: External hordeolum is usually self-limiting; first-line management is warm compresses and lid hygiene, with topical antibiotics reserved for associated conjunctivitis or discharge. Advise against squeezing.
